attributes.stem: A 38-year-old man being investigated for epigastric pain and a mild anaemia. The pain is worse after meals and he gets some relief with an antacid. He has no other symptoms. He is not on any medications, does not smoke and drinks about two cans of beer a day. The physical examination is unremarkable. His haemoglobin is 128 g/L (135-175). An endoscopy is performed and nothing abnormal is found in the oesophagus or stomach. This is the view seen in the second part of the duodenum and a protuberance is noted on the medial wall at about the same level as the pancreas (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following should be done next?
attributes.explanation:
Seen here is the hepatopancreatic ampulla (Ampulla of Vater). This is normal and should be left alone. The ampulla is a protrusion of the biliary tree and connects the gastrointestinal tract to the pancreas, liver and gallbladder.

Biopsy, endoscopic mucosal resection, or snare and excise would be inappropriate decisions. These are viable options for abnormalities of the gastrointestinal tract such as polyps or tumours. Only if the ampulla appeared abnormal would it be biopsied. The hepatopancreatic ampulla is normal anatomy and should not be disturbed in the absence of any clinical indication. A sphincterotomy could be performed at this site, should it be necessary to access the biliary tree during endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) for stone retrieval.

Malignancies of the biliary and pancreatic trees are possible and one way to assess these is with brush cytology, however this would only be indicated if a malignancy was already expected eg. with the presence of stricture of the bile ducts.

The presence of the hepatopancreatic ampulla is not related to this patient's epigastric pain and mild anaemia. This piece of anatomy is usually clearly visible during endoscopic procedures.
learningPoints: The hepatopancreatic ampulla is a part of normal gastrointestinal anatomy , is located medially at the second part of the duodenum, and to the untrained eye may appear as an abnormality.
references: Romiti A, Barucca V, Zullo A, Sarcina I, Di Rocco R, D’Antonio C, Latorre M, Marchetti P. Tumors of ampulla of Vater: a case series and review of chemotherapy options. World journal of gastrointestinal oncology. 2012 Mar 3;4(3):60.
